headlines.title: Watch live: Authorities provide update on massive warehouse fire in Tracy
headlines.description: A medical supply warehouse was destroyed after it went up in flames and prompted evacuations of neighboring buildings Thursday afternoon in Tracy. The massive blaze, which was one of several fires reported on the 5700 block of Promotory Parkway in the San Joaquin County city, could be seen from across the Bay Area region. Firefighters pointed to water supply challenges and weather conditions as the reason the blaze spread quickly and engulfed the approximately 1 million-square-foot facility. No injuries have been reported, officials said. Officers were also on scene assisting with evacuations and directing traffic, according to police. A police spokesperson said explosions can be heard at the warehouse as flames continued to burn. Police have also told residents impacted by the smoke to close their windows and to shelter in place. Medline Industries warehouse destroyed in Tracy fire NBC Bay Area’s SkyRanger showed the gutted building belonged to Medline Industries. The company has a distribution center located in the area. Medline provided the following statement: “Medline can confirm a fire is occurring at our Tracy, California distribution facility. All Medline employees and on-site personnel are accounted for. The safety and well-being of our employees is our top priority. We are coordinating closely with local authorities and first responders as we assess the fire’s impact and will share more details as they become available.” Fire breaks out at neighboring FedEx warehouse after blaze prompted evacuations Tracy Mayor Dan Arriola said there was no mandatory evacuations in place due to the blaze, but added several nearby businesses in the area self-evacuated. Police said all warehouses southwest of the Medline building have been evacuated: “We are aware of multiple fires in the area and are coordinating with personnel from South San Joaquin County Fire Authority to assess conditions and address any evacuation needs if they arise. Out of an abundance of caution, public safety personnel are present in the Tracy Hills area; however, there is currently no threat to residents. Personnel are assisting with traffic control and monitoring conditions.” The neighboring warehouse evacuated during the blaze is FedEx’s Tracy hub. The company provided the following statement: “We are aware of a building fire that started at a neighboring location before spreading to the Tracy hub. All team members have been safely evacuated and are accounted for. As this is an active situation, further questions should be directed to the local authorities.” At around 7 p.m., authorities said an additional fire broke out at the nearby FedEx warehouse, burning pallets and multiple big rig trailers. Officials said firefighters “performed an aggressive fire attack” to bring it under control. They added that the exterior of building caught fire, but the flames did not engulf the building. Tracy’s city manager declares local state of emergency As around 200 fire personnel combat the fire, authorities said the city’s manager declared a local state of emergency to help ensure that all available resources and emergency response capabilities are available to assist. How did the fire spread so quickly in the large Tracy warehouse? The blaze was reported just before 1 p.m. When firefighters arrived, they found a “rapidly developing fire involving the roof” of the warehouse. Officials said firefighters faced water supply challenges with no water pressure in the building. In addition, sprinklers installed in the warehouse were not working. “Early suppression efforts were hampered by a failure of the building’s private fire water system, which significantly impacted initial firefighting operations,” the City of Tracy said in a statement. “Combined with high winds, low humidity, and elevated temperatures, the fire quickly extended from the roof into the interior of the facility.” A cause of the fire is still under investigation, police said. How could the warehouse fire impact air quality in surrounding areas? According to the San Joaquin Valley Air Quality District, as of Thursday afternoon there was no indications the smoke was impacting the air quality, because the smoke is high and up off the ground. However, authorities said as the night falls and temperatures dop, there is concern the smoke could fall as well. Because of this, they advised residents to go inside their homes if they smell smoke, keep their doors and windows shut and run their HVAC. If they don’t have a place to go, officials advised them to go to any place with air conditioning. The warning comes as authorities said they don’t know what’s in the smoke, so they said, “it’s best not to breathe it.” The San Joaquin Valley Air Quality District said they will keep monitoring the air quality in the neighborhood near the fire throughout the night. Warehouse fire sparks mutual aid response In addition to Tracy firefighters and Cal Fire crews, several fire departments from the Bay Area responded to the massive warehouse blaze. The Alameda County Fire Department sent over a strike team and additional resources to the blaze. The Contra Costa County Fire Protection District sent over two engines to help provide coverage in the city. Napa County Feb 24 Alameda County May 22, 2025 This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
headlines.source: Kristofer Noceda, John Zuchelli, Jodi Hernandez and Victoria Meza

headlines.title: Thousands pack downtown San Jose for World Cup kickoff
headlines.description: The party is still going strong in downtown San Jose after thousands of soccer fans packed San Pedro Square to watch Mexico defeat South Africa in the opening match of the FIFA World Cup on Thursday. The historic kickoff of the global tournament brought a sea of bright green Mexico national team jerseys to the South Bay, with some fans arriving before 8 a.m.—four hours before the noon match began. The celebrations are expected to continue into the evening as fans prepare for the next tournament match between South Korea and Czechia, which kicks off at 7 p.m. Thursday. During the opening match, the crowd was overwhelmingly unified. Virtually no South African jerseys could be spotted in the square as local fans gathered to cheer on El Tri. For many, the tournament opener was reason enough to put their daily routines on hold. “No work today. Today Mexico plays,” said Jesus Villasano, a Mexico fan who skipped work for the event. “Party day. Party day. We waited four years for this.” The festive atmosphere featured a live mariachi band playing traditional songs for the crowd of Mexican and Mexican American fans. The massive turnout also brought a welcome wave of customers to downtown businesses, drawing a visit from San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan. “There’s a lot of people who called in sick today, Mayor,” noted NBC Bay Area reporter Damian Trujillo during the broadcast. “That’s right. It’s World Cup, guys,” Mahan responded. “You get a free pass today.” Mahan said the event represents a major economic boost for local restaurants and bar owners, as well as a significant victory for regional sports fans. More than 20,000 people have RSVP’d for the opening-day watch party at San Pedro Square, according to the San Jose Earthquakes, which is co-hosting the event. Organizers have erected giant 27-by-13-foot television screens in the middle of the square, while local bars and restaurants prepare for packed houses. The local events coincide with the Bay Area’s role as a World Cup host. Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara — temporarily renamed San Francisco Bay Area Stadium for the tournament due to FIFA sponsorship regulations — will host six World Cup matches, starting with Qatar versus Switzerland on Saturday. For fans who do not have tickets to the stadium matches, local organizers are trying to recreate the atmosphere in public spaces. “It’s incredible because you can feel it,” said Gary Peralta of San Jose. “When you know there’s a stadium, you can feel it, but also you’re having watch parties for games, so it’s like you’re there anyway.” “Any time you get people to come down in the vicinity of your business, you have a time to shine,” said Douglas Cookerly, vice president of operations for Sushi Confidential. “You have an opportunity to generate new customers, you give the existing customers interaction, it’s really about all of it together.” The San Pedro Square site will broadcast every match of the tournament. However, city organizers have scheduled various other free, public watch parties across San Jose to highlight the city’s diverse cultural neighborhoods: — Eastridge Center and the Mexican Heritage Plaza will broadcast all Mexico matches. — The Japantown Night Market will feature Japan versus Sweden, as well as the United States versus Turkey match. — Santana Row on Winchester Boulevard will host a viewing for the Colombia versus Congo match later this month. — The Plant on Monterey Highway will broadcast the Round of 32 matches. — Alum Rock Village will host quarterfinal viewings. — Little Saigon will show the playoff rounds and the World Cup final.

headlines.title: Some World Cup fans say they are frustrated by FIFA's ticketing and customer service
headlines.description: Several soccer fans who paid thousands of dollars for World Cup tickets say they are frustrated with FIFA’s ticketing process, prompting complaints to NBC Los Angeles and scrutiny from state attorneys general. Long Beach resident Joe Mello said he bought four tickets to the Belgium-Iran match at SoFi Stadium, paying $1,600. But only one ticket initially appeared in his FIFA app, leaving him worried as the Father’s Day game approached. 2026 World Cup Jun 9 “The frustration was not knowing where I was sitting, not knowing if they were going to get it fixed in time and not being able to talk to anyone who seemed to know what they were doing,” Mello said. Mello said FIFA eventually fixed the problem after he spent about a week calling and emailing the organization. He said he believes the situation could have become a “disaster” at the stadium had he not been persistent. Other fans say their complaints center not on missing tickets, but on the seats they received. Jose Flores, a soccer fan from Downey, said he and his wife spent $3,800 on two tickets to Los Angeles’ first World Cup match, the United States against Paraguay. Flores said they purchased Category 2 tickets, which FIFA describes on its website as seats “positioned outside of Category 1 areas” and available in both lower and upper tiers. Flores said he expected midlevel seats. Instead, he said, FIFA assigned them to Section 512 at SoFi Stadium, near the top of the venue. “We knew we were going to get OK seats, but we were not expecting to be seated in the 500s section,” Flores said. Flores said he complained to FIFA but was told his seats would not be reassigned. He said the situation became more frustrating when he later saw Category 2 tickets closer to the field listed on FIFA’s website for less than he paid. “Those same Category 2 tickets you can find in the lower section for $800 cheaper,” Flores said. “And that part is extremely frustrating.” NBC Los Angeles contacted FIFA multiple times for comment but did not receive a response. Derek Howard, a longtime trial attorney who teaches sports law at the University of San Francisco, said ticketholders who believe they were misled could consult a consumer attorney. But he said legal action may not move quickly enough to get fans different seats before match day. Howard said fans may get faster attention by filing complaints with the state attorney general’s office. “Here in California, this falls right within the wheelhouse of the attorney general,” Howard said. California Attorney General Rob Bonta’s office said he recently sent FIFA a letter requesting detailed information about its ticketing practices. In the letter, Bonta said California provides legal protections for consumers and has “strict prohibitions on marketing practices that are likely to mislead them.” Bonta’s office is encouraging ticketholders to file complaints, though a spokesperson declined to say how many complaints have been received. California is not the only state examining FIFA’s ticketing practices. The attorneys general of New York and New Jersey have announced a formal investigation. “Being honest about ticket sales is not complicated,” New Jersey Attorney General Matthew Platkin said in a statement. “But FIFA has turned buying a ticket to the World Cup into a gauntlet of confusion, fake scarcity, and impossibly high prices — all at the expense of consumers.” For now, Howard recommends that fans with ticketing issues keep contacting FIFA and document their efforts. Mello said he had to search extensively before finding a way to reach a person at FIFA. “I did an AI search, and it came up with the FIFA information and the cheat codes on how to actually talk to a human, which came in really handy,” he said. Flores said he and his wife still plan to attend the match, despite their frustration. “We’ll have to go to the game, bite the bullet and then see what happens,” he said.

headlines.title: CaliBunga Waterpark in San Jose will not open this summer, source says
headlines.description: CaliBunga Waterpark in San Jose will not be opening this summer, according to a source. The water park, formerly known as Raging Waters, will temporarily close as the city looks to reimagine and expand the facility. The city will be working with another partner to make plans and improvements to get the water park back open in the future. The park will no longer operate under the CaliBunga name. Lake Cunningham Regional Park will remain open and may have additional activities this summer. San Jose Jul 4, 2024 San Jose May 10, 2024 This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Oakland beloved sports bar reopens just in time for the World Cup
headlines.description: Oakland sports fans packed the sidewalks Thursday as one of the city’s most beloved sports bars made its return. Soccer fans aren’t just cheering for their favorite team — they’re celebrating the return of a beloved Oakland sports bar. Less than an hour after opening its doors, the Oakland Athletic Club hit capacity, with fans lining up down the block to get inside. “We missed them and we want them to stay open and stay open in business as long as they can. For us, it was to show support for the World Cup but also for them,” said Fracisco Alvarado of Oakland. “The spirit of Oakland is the spirit of the tailgate,” Oakland Athletic Club co-owner Angela Tsay said. The bar closed in February, but a new local ownership group made up of Oakland business owners stepped in to bring it back. Tsay, who also owns Oaklandish, explains they want to keep Oakland’s sports tradition alive while building a year-round gathering place for the community. “I think it’s important for people to see that there are still people who are willing to make a small bet on Oakland, who believe in Oakland, we want to continue to invest in Oakland and the more we do that, the more it will attract other people and entrepreneurs and investors,” Tsay said. Opening day coincided with the start of the FIFA World Cup, turning the bar’s comeback into a celebration for both soccer fans and Oakland supporters. “It’s very joyful, people greeting each other, like none of us know each other but we are introducing ourselves and just talking about football,” Alvarado said. The Oakland Athletic Club is also the official bar partner of the Oakland Roots and Soul and the Oakland Ballers. While sports is still the main attraction, owners say they’ve rebuilt the kitchen and revamped the food and drink menu, hoping the food becomes as much of a draw as the action on the screens. “We want it to be somewhere that you come here, even not just on game day but for lunch or gameday with a friend, and that the food is a draw,” Tsay said. Visit Oakland CEO Peter Gamez believes the reopening will help drive more visitors to Oakland for day and overnight stays and sends a positive message about the city’s future. “They believe in the town,” Gamez said. “I think other business owners see hope there, they see opportunity and they see a light at the end of the tunnel that our downtown is revitalizing.”

headlines.title: A closer look at how the World Cup impacts local businesses in the South Bay
headlines.description: The big watch party downtown San Jose, as well as the neighborhood gatherings throughout the South Bay, are geared to help fans gather for the games and also to boost local business. The cheers at the Santana Row World Cup watch party meant Mexico got their goal, and so did the Bay Area Host Committee. The BAHC and San Jose are both organizing neighborhood watch parties to give fans a place to gather and to boost local businesses, now and in the long run. This worked at Santana Row, where a FIFA pop-up store is scoring well with fans. “The business has been great, yeah, we’ve seen a lot of people come in and we’ll be here until the end of July even after the event is over we will be here after that,” store manager Manny Bhagat said. It was equally loud and enthusiastic, and even more crowded, at the San Jose watch party at the Eastridge mall. Dilbreet Bhandal was enjoying the game, splitting time at the watch party, preparing food at his two businesses: Great Kahn’s and a Subway. He says the strategy to boost local businesses is working. “More and more people are starting to come in. As you see, in the morning, there wasn’t that many people and now, it’s so many people here, and so much excitement everywhere,” Bhandal said. “I think when people hear about what we’re doing, whether it’s partnering with our small businesses, or hosting these gatherings, there’s a lot of excitement for people to come together,” San Jose City Councilmember Domingo Candelas said. Diana Zepeda, a World Cup fan, said the plan makes sense, and it encourages her to go out and shop local businesses more. “I just got my eyebrows done downstairs and now I’m grabbing food, so I’m heading back to watch the game. Super excited to be here.” A lot of people were sticking around and shopping; the first step to get people to frequent their local businesses now and after the games are over.

headlines.title: San Bruno man builds system to track planes on his home ceiling
headlines.description: A San Bruno man built a system to track planes on his ceiling as they approach his home. Cameron Paczek, an engineer, built a system he calls “Skylight”. it’s open source, and tracks flights as they approach his San Bruno home. The system shows planes coming and going on the ceiling of his house. Using a small Raspberry Pi Computer, a projector, and a radio antenna, Paczek has created what might be the ultimate conversation starter. NBC Bay Area’s Scott Budman has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Teens arrested and accused of stealing Pokemón cards in San Francisco
headlines.description: Two teens have been arrested after police say they stole Pokémon cards in San Francisco. It happened on May 28. San Francisco police said the victim agreed to meet a public park to sell his collection of trading cards, but instead of paying, one of the two teens pepper-sprayed him, took his cards and got away in a car driven by another teen. Officers used license plate cameras to track the thief and the driver. The next day, they were both arrested and booked into Juvenile Hall.

headlines.title: SF's New Mission theater to be named after Christopher Nolan
headlines.description: San Francisco’s New Mission theater will be renamed after critically acclaimed movie director Christopher Nolan to honor his contributions to filmmaking as one of the pioneers in large format movie presentations. The movie theater chain Alamo Drafthouse Cinema began operating out of the New Mission theater in 2015 and the renaming is part of the 110th anniversary of the building, which first opened its doors to moviegoers May 4, 1916. The renaming announced Wednesday also comes ahead of Nolan’s highly anticipated film, “The Odyssey,” releasing on July 17. “San Francisco’s New Mission is a beautiful, historic, iconic cinema with more than a century of rich history,” Nolan said in a statement. “To be recognized in this way by Alamo Drafthouse, and with this particular theater which continues the great tradition of showing films on 70mm film, is an incredible honour.” The theater received its now iconic design during a 1932 remodel by Timothy Pflueger. In 2001, the building was placed on the National Register of Historic Places. The theater has undergone various upgrades to interior design elements over the years. Upgrades include bringing back the original paint palette and 1930s carpeting. The theater was also equipped with a new full kitchen and bar as a part of Alamo Drafthouse moving into the space. Nolan joins a list of other important filmmakers whose names are on Alamo Drafthouse locations. Spike Lee, Bong Joon Ho and John Hughes have received similar treatment. Official commemoration for this honor will happen later this year at a 70mm screening event in San Francisco that Nolan will attend. “By dedicating New Mission in Mr. Nolan’s name, we’re celebrating and thanking a filmmaker whose work has consistently championed cinema not merely as entertainment, but as a cultural inheritance worth protecting,” Michael Kustermann, Alamo Drafthouse CEO, said in a statement.
headlines.source: Bay City News | Lloyd B. Davis

headlines.title: Complaints pile up over Waymo cars taking over San Francisco parking garage
headlines.description: Residents of a San Francisco condominium community say it’s been frustrating and terrible to share a parking garage with Waymo vehicles. The residents at SoMa Grand, located at 1160 Mission St., say the volume of driverless cars in the garage and the occasional quirks when the cars get confused can add up to several minutes of time wasted. “Every morning it takes me a while to get out because it’s always traffic with these Waymos,” one resident, Margarita, said. Margarita said she was late to work after getting stuck in the garage for 20 minutes because of the Waymos. Another resident said their biggest concern is when they get stuck behind two or three Waymos inside the garage and one of the Waymo cars gets confused. The residents then have to wait for a Waymo technician to come out and clear the congestion. Another resident, Paul Dack, said it’s also bad news when they get stuck behind Waymos coming into the garage. “Last week I came, there was three stuck here trying to get into the entrance and nobody was moving forever,” he said. “Once they start getting up the ramp, then you’re stuck behind three of them to get all the way to the top.” Waymo has been operating at the garage since last year, but residents and Waymo did not say exactly when the autonomous car operator set up shop. Waymo also did not share how many of its cars operate out of the location. In a statement, a Waymo spokesperson said, in part, “We care deeply about the communities we serve and pride ourselves on being a good neighbor.” The spokesperson also said Waymo has made improvements since first moving into the garage, including deploying more parking staff to help direct residents, installing more charging stations for residents, and installing speed bumps and mirrors at some garage corners for driver safety. At least one of those improvements has actually caused more frustration for Margarita. “Speed bumps, yeah, like every couple feet,” she said. “Also it’s very bad for your car and it takes time to go through all this situation. Yeah, the situation is terrible.” NBC Bay Area also reached out to Ace Parking, the company that manages the garage, and the condominium manager but did not immediately hear back.

headlines.title: Arrest made in Berkeley Animal Shelter arson investigation
headlines.description: A man was arrested and accused of igniting a fire that closed the Berkeley Animal Shelter over the weekend, the police department announced Thursday. Berkeley police said they arrested 48-year-old Peter Taraba on suspicion of arson and animal cruelty. According to authorities, the fire ocurred on Saturday at around 10:08 p.m., and on Wednesday, investigators found evidence indicating the fire may have been intentionally set. Police said property crime detectives reviewed surveillance footage, and based on evidence recovered from the area, they determined the suspect used a rock to break the window and ignited the fire using an “improvised incendiary device.” Authorities reported they identified Taraba as the suspect when he came back to the area. Taraba was arrested on Thursday morning in downtown Berkeley.

headlines.title: State, Santa Clara County sue Trump Administration over planned ICE facility
headlines.description: The state of California and Santa Clara County are again suing the administration of President Donald Trump, this time for purported plans for a facility operated by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement near Gilroy. The exact use of the planned facility still has not been confirmed by county officials, but demolition on the site at 7240 Holsclaw Road began in recent weeks, which convinced Santa Clara County Counsel Tony LoPresti, the Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors, and the California Department of Justice that recent reporting about a planned facility from local media outlet San Jose Spotlight was accurate. The county and state Department of Justice contend that the roughly 25-acre property is exclusively zoned for agricultural use and could not accommodate the water, sewer and other infrastructure needed for a holding facility, according to LoPresti. They further argued that the federal administration had not been in contact with local or state officials at all, including for legally required public feedback on any proposed project and completing and submitting environmental impact reports. LoPresti said unpermitted demolition underway on the site could release hazardous materials and said the lawsuit was seeking an injunction to immediately stop the work, although he could not offer a specific timeline when asked by a reporter when would be soonest that could occur. The state has been a party in over 70 lawsuits against the administration seeking to stop a range of policies it contended were unconstitutional or otherwise illegal, including frequent bouts over the withholding of a range of federal grant money, according to Attorney General Rob Bonta. A spokesperson for ICE previously denied that whatever planned facility was being leased from the property owner by the U.S. General Services Administration–which executes contracts for agencies across the federal government–would be used as a detention facility. The agency did not respond to a request for comment on the lawsuit or project’s progress on Wednesday afternoon by press time. But blueprints for the planned structure were made public last week that indicated holding rooms for up to 150 people, according to LoPresti. The possible holding rooms are one of the aspects of the project that is prohibited by the county’s zoning regulations, LoPresti said at a press conference Wednesday alongside Bonta. The contract award from the General Services Administration outlines a roughly $26 million contract to lease a yet-to-be-built 18,700-square-foot office building on the property for 20 years. It includes plans for 60 parking spaces and was awarded in the waning days of the administration of then-president Joe Biden on Jan. 8, 2025, records show. LoPresti said local officials reacted with “outrage that the federal government would construct a facility with such a massive impact with no stakeholder input whatsoever.” A request for a preliminary injunction to halt the work and cancel the project was filed Wednesday in the San Jose Division of the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. It argues that the project is inconsistent with the area’s land use and would be infeasible on the surrounding agricultural and rural roads and because of the inadequate septic system on the property. It further argues that the property is in an area susceptible to flooding, making any potential septic issues dangerous for the environment and detainees that would be held there. “The development of this kind of facility in disregard of applicable laws threatens to subject detainees to inhumane and unhealthy conditions, which have already tainted the federal government’s immigration enforcement operations across the country,” the request reads. Bonta said his office had conducted an evaluation of federal immigration detention facilities people were being were held in in California and found that “the conditions of detention are absolutely horrific and unacceptable.” He said there were “basic human needs not being provided to the detainees, and most of the detainees shouldn’t even be in detention.” LoPresti said the project violated federal laws including the National Environmental Policy Act, the Intergovernmental Cooperation Act, Immigration and Nationality Act, and the Administrative Procedure Act. He also argued it violated the state’s Williamson Act, which allows properties to be zoned for certain restricted uses and receive property tax breaks in return. The property at 7240 Holdsclaw was zoned for agricultural use in 1967 and has received tax breaks ever since, according to the civil complaint. The current owner, Beverly Hills-based ECG 6 LLC, purchased the property on Jan. 30, 2025, and the complaint alleges that it was aware of the restrictions and requirements to notify the county about any plan to change its use or build without needed permits. LoPresti also said community members were right to suspect that the presence of a detention or processing facility could lead to more federal enforcement in the region, saying that had been the pattern elsewhere and was something officials wanted to prevent. Neighboring Monterey County reacted to the news by directing its own county government to ensure zoning regulations there could be used as a way to prevent a similar episode from occurring. Gov. Gavin Newsom also weighed in after the lawsuit was announced, saying on social media that the administration’s immigration policies were creating fear and hurting communities. “State and local leaders will not stop fighting for Californians in the face of federal overreach and terror,” he wrote on X. “At a time when communities are already living in fear, the Trump Admin is constructing this ICE facility while blatantly disregarding basic legal requirements and local concerns,” Newsom said.
